title: HTTP Gateway
description: REST gateway, management endpoints, auth, and generated client

blocks.text: Hound includes a built-in HTTP gateway that exposes emit and management endpoints over REST. It lets external services emit jobs without a direct Redis connection and provides a browser-friendly management interface.
Start the gateway via hound.listen(). Pass a HoundManagement instance to also expose the management endpoints:
Or use createGateway() for more control over hostname and options:
These endpoints are always available, regardless of whether management is enabled:

rows: Emit a single job — body: { event, data?, options? }
Emit multiple jobs — body: [{ event, data?, options? }]

blocks.text:
curl -X POST http://localhost:4000/emit \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer my-secret-token' \
  -d '{"event": "email.send", "data": {"to": "leo@example.com"}}'
Available when a HoundManagement instance is passed to the gateway:
Set the auth option on Hound.create() to require a Bearer token on all gateway requests:
Clients must include the token in the Authorization header:
Without auth, any service that can reach the gateway port can emit jobs and access management endpoints.
Hound can generate a typed TypeScript client from your registered job definitions. The client wraps the HTTP gateway and provides the same type safety as direct hound.emit() calls:
The generated client is self-contained — no Hound or Redis dependency. Import it anywhere to emit jobs over HTTP:

import { houndClient } from './gen/hound-client.ts';

await houndClient.emit('email.send', { to: 'leo@example.com' });
